summary: Gruber argues that iOS fundamentally lacks consistent, discoverable conventions for basic commands like Undo and Redo, using Apple's own App Store awards as evidence. He notes that Apple's editorial story on Procreate Pocket devotes an entire paragraph to explaining how to invoke Undo, something unthinkable for a Mac app where ⌘Z has been universal since 1984. He traces the problem to iOS's lack of a structural equivalent to the Mac menu bar, which provided a consistent home for commands across all applications. The piece contends that Shake to Undo is both awful and undiscoverable, forcing each app developer to reinvent basic interaction patterns. Gruber concludes that iOS, after eleven years, is still floundering to establish conventions the Mac solved in its first year.
key_points: Apple's own App Store editorial inadvertently indicts iOS conventions by needing to explain Undo in a seven-paragraph story about Procreate Pocket
The Mac established universal Undo/Redo conventions in 1984 that have remained consistent for decades, while iOS has no reliable equivalent
Shake to Undo is so bad that developers are forced to choose between consuming screen space with buttons, inventing custom gestures, or relying on an interaction most users hate or don't know exists
The menu bar is a vastly underestimated foundation of desktop computing whose absence on iOS leaves the platform without a structural home for consistent commands
Apple's iWork apps on iOS effectively reinvent the menu bar behind a '···' button while refusing to admit they need one
money_quotes: This is — inadvertently on the part of the App Store editorial team — a scathing indictment of the state of iOS's user interface standards.
Before reading a word of it, how much would you wager that Apple's story on Pixelmator Pro for Mac does not mention how to invoke Undo and Redo? I would've bet my house.
Undo has been in the same position in the same menu with the same keyboard shortcut since 1984.

iOS user interface conventions are so shallow, so widely and wildly inconsistent, that an app proclaimed by Apple as the very best of the year has to start, as the very first thing you see when you launch them, by teaching you how to use Undo. That's a sad state of affairs.
Once heralded, the menu bar is now seen as a vestige.
iOS is an 11-year-old platform that is still floundering to establish consistent conventions for some basic features, let alone complex ones, that are simple and obvious on the Mac.
Imagine going back in time to tell a MacPaint user in 1985 that they'd have to learn how to use Undo in an Apple-award-winning paint app in 2018. That's where we are.
key_insight: The absence of a menu bar equivalent on iOS has left the platform without a structural foundation for consistent, discoverable commands — a problem the Mac solved in 1984 that iOS still hasn't addressed after eleven years.
